prevalence.trend: stable to increasing in urban areas

vulnerablePopulations: low-income families with limited access to emollients and dermatology care
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis based on pruritic eczema with typical distribution and chronic/relapsing course; validated criteria (e.g., UK Working Party) used in research; rule out scabies and contact dermatitis

publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; basic control possible, severe disease requires specialist access

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: APS manages mild cases; dermatology referral for moderate-severe disease
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emollients, topical corticosteroids/calcineurin inhibitors; phototherapy and systemic immunomodulators for severe disease; biologics (e.g., dupilumab) limited to specialized access
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Access to emollients and specialists; limited biologic availability in SUS
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Primary care coverage and availability of topical corticosteroids in SUS
